8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Ion (Ivan) Sipilov on UnsplashThe ladder didn’t get harder to climb. Someone removed the bottom rung and left the sign up.
A design lead at a mid-sized product company told me she posted a junior role last year and received something north of a thousand applications. I asked who she hired. She said a designer with six years of experience who took a pay cut to get in the door. I asked whether she changed the title afterward. She said no, it still says junior.
So the listing was real. The rung was not.
That gap is the entire story of what happened to entry-level design, and almost nobody is describing it accurately.
Every honest report will tell you the design market stabilized. That is true. What the reports do not say loudly enough is where it stabilized. It stabilized above the people who needed it most. Senior roles recovered. Generalist roles recovered. Team sizes stopped shrinking. And underneath all of that recovery, the first job in a design career quietly stopped existing as a functional category, while continuing to exist as a job title.
Juniors are not losing a competition. They are applying to a position that no longer does what the position was invented to do.
The Problem
